teamObjective.explanation: My hand is very weak (3-6 of spades, all non-trump), so I cannot expect to keep the lead for long. The best team objective is to play the lowest card to invite my partner to take control of the trick.

partnerBelief.summary: My partner has 3 unknown cards and is likely to hold at least one card higher than 3 or a trump, but I cannot be certain. A low lead signals weakness and asks partner to cover if possible without overcommitting high value cards.

opponentBelief.summary: Each opponent holds 3 unknown cards. Given the deck distribution, it is probable that at least one opponent has a card higher than a low single such as 3 or a trump. Opponents will likely try to seize the lead early.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of spades is the weakest legal play. It signals to my partner that I have a weak hand, gives partner the cheapest chance to take the lead, and preserves the 6 as my strongest remaining card for a later trick or to cover an opponent's low lead.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Playing the 4 wastes a card that is still unlikely to win the trick outright, and it gives less room for partner to cover cheaply.
The 5 is my second-best card; losing it now to a higher opponent card would strip my hand of a card that might win a future trick or beat a low opponent lead.

Leading the 6 is the most aggressive option but is still very vulnerable to any 7 or higher non-trump, trump-rank, or actual trump card. If an opponent covers, I lose my only high card and retain little control.

risks: An opponent may play a higher single and take the lead.
My partner may be unable or unwilling to cover, leaving the opponents in control.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By keeping the 6 as my best card, I retain some ability to regain control or respond to a low opponent lead on a later trick.

notes: No public cards have been played, so all beliefs are probabilistic and grounded only in the new_game event and current hand counts.
